narrative: As an Asian male applicant, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on data from thousands of students who took the bar exam, focusing on factors like academic records, test scores, and background to predict first-time pass rates. The model assessed your profile— including your undergraduate GPA of 3.40, LSAT score of 26, upper-middle family income, full-time student status, and notably low Year 1 and Year 3 school grade deciles of 1—and predicted a 53.4% probability that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt. This tipped the scales just over the threshold, leading to the rejection of your application as the school prioritizes admitting students likely to succeed on the bar. The most influential factor pushing toward this failure prediction was your Year 3 school grade decile, which ranked you at the bottom of your class and strongly signaled potential struggles ahead compared to peers who typically rank much higher, around the middle of their classes. Next, your undergraduate GPA worked in your favor, as your solid 3.40 exceeds the average of 3.21 and pulled the prediction somewhat toward passing. Your upper-middle family income also helped mildly, nudging things positively since it aligns with brackets that correlate with better outcomes on average. While your low LSAT score of 26—well below the typical 36.7—and bottom-decile early grades contributed to the overall concern, it was primarily that Year 3 performance that carried the heaviest weight in the model's eyes. Compared to typical admitted students, who boast stronger year-end rankings and higher entrance exam scores, your profile stood out as riskier despite strengths like your GPA and stable background. This doesn't define your potential, especially as an Asian male where many overcome early hurdles through targeted effort. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score toward the average, seeking academic advising to improve class rankings in future studies, and gathering letters from mentors who can highlight your resilience. Building a stronger narrative around your full-time commitment and family support could also shine in reapplying to other programs. With focused improvements, you can shift the odds in your favor for the next cycle.
